summary: Cathy O'Neil writes a lighthearted vacation dispatch from Stockholm, explaining her blogging hiatus through the lens of cultural contrast. She describes falling into a 'Northern European funk' where the relaxed Swedish pace has her knitting instead of writing. She humorously critiques Stockholm's obsession with queuing while admiring the country's beautiful, friendly people and social systems. The post uses a Star Trek analogy to position Sweden as a utopia, while acknowledging she's only seeing the privileged side and not the experiences of unemployed immigrant youth in the suburbs.
key_points: The relaxed Swedish atmosphere stands in stark contrast to New York's tension and energy, affecting productivity and mindset
Swedish queuing culture represents a fundamentally different relationship with time than American impatience
Sweden appears utopian with its universal healthcare and gorgeous citizenry, but this view is limited to privileged neighborhoods
The author acknowledges her perspective is from 'the Upper West Side equivalent of Stockholm' rather than the immigrant suburbs experiencing unrest
money_quotes: Being here in gorgeous sunny Stockholm is kind of like lying back in a cloud.
It's so soft, so bereft of the natural tension and energy of New York, that you just feel like knitting all day and drinking coffee.
They don't seem to value their time like we do in New York.
Naturally I felt incredibly honored to be taking part in such a hallowed ritual, as I explained to him.
I'd have to put Stockholm and its ridiculously gorgeous citizenry firmly on the utopia end of the Star Trek spectrum.
Of course I say that while temporarily residing in the Upper West Side equivalent of Stockholm, and not as an unemployed immigrant youth in the suburbs.
key_insight: Even apparent utopias look different depending on where you're standing within them.
